meta.title: TriMetrix HD & The Mindset Model
meta.source: Adapted from the VisionOne Mindset Reference Guide and the TTI Personal Talent Skills Inventory / Axiology (Vera Mefford). For coaching use.
meta.premise: The average person has 12,000–15,000 subconscious, habitual thoughts per day. Neuroplasticity means we can reorganize how we think. How you think is not who you are — you can take your thoughts captive and change them.

dimensions.name: The Value of Others (Empathy)
dimensions.question: Who are they as an individual?
dimensions.over: People's needs come before action and rules.

dimensions.name: The Value of Action (Practical Thinking)
dimensions.question: What do I need to do?
dimensions.over: Getting things done comes before people and rules.
dimensions.under: Getting things done matters less than people or rules.

dimensions.name: The Value of Order (Systems Judgment)
dimensions.question: What is the framework or idea?
dimensions.over: Systems, rules & authority outweigh needs and action.
dimensions.under: Individual needs and action outweigh the need for order.

dimensions.name: The Value of Self (Sense of Self)
dimensions.question: Who am I?
dimensions.over: Who I am matters more than what I do or am becoming.
dimensions.under: My worth matters less than what I do or am becoming.

dimensions.name: The Value of Roles (Role Awareness)
dimensions.question: What are my roles?
dimensions.over: My role matters more than who I am and am becoming.
dimensions.under: My current roles matter less than who I am becoming.

dimensions.name: The Value of Potential (Self-Direction)
dimensions.question: What is my ideal self?
dimensions.over: My future self matters more than who I am and what I do.
dimensions.under: My future matters less than who I am or what I do today.

distortions.name: Personalization
distortions.distortion: Taking the blame for whatever is wrong; owning what you can't control; assuming everything said is directed at you.
distortions.truth: There are multiple perspectives to every situation; not everything that's wrong needs a person to blame.
reversing: What if this isn't about you? What would you do differently?
Let's focus on what you CAN control.

distortions.name: Control Fallacy
distortions.distortion: Feeling either helpless (no control) or completely in control of yourself and surroundings.
distortions.truth: You always have some level of control, and some things can't be controlled.
reversing: What CAN you control?
How would you perceive this differently if it weren't about you?

distortions.name: Blaming
distortions.distortion: Blaming self or others; blame gives a false sense of control and permits heightened emotions.
distortions.truth: Blame doesn't reach the root problem and blocks empathy; you're never in complete control.
reversing: How does finding blame lead to the solution?
What CAN you control, and what can you let go of?

distortions.name: Fallacy of Change
distortions.distortion: Expecting that pressure will make someone change; believing another's success is because of you.
distortions.truth: External pressure never produces lasting change; you're not responsible for making someone change.
reversing: What's the purpose of this change and why does it matter to you?
What if you aren't responsible for making them change?

distortions.name: Heaven's Reward
distortions.distortion: Expecting reward for sacrifice; believing a force is “keeping score,” becoming bitter when unrewarded.
distortions.truth: Do what's right regardless of outcome; self-deprecation isn't true humility.
reversing: What's the right thing to do, regardless of outcome?
If you never get praise, what will you do differently?

distortions.name: Emotional Reasoning
distortions.distortion: Letting emotions define truth; believing feelings are automatically true; ignoring contradicting facts.
distortions.truth: Feelings aren't always true; you can't think rationally under heightened emotion.
reversing: What can you do to diffuse the emotion right now?
What facts support or contradict the feeling?

distortions.name: Over-Generalization
distortions.distortion: Broad generalizations — “everyone,” “always,” “never” — based on past experience.
distortions.truth: People, tasks and systems have similarities but aren't all the same; change is the only constant.
reversing: What's unique about this person or situation?
What if it isn't always true — what would you do differently?

distortions.name: Jumping to Conclusions
distortions.distortion: Making negative assumptions without evidence; trying to read minds or foretell the future.
distortions.truth: It's best to ask rather than read minds; the past isn't an accurate predictor.
reversing: What are the facts? What do you KNOW to be true?
What assumptions are you making?

distortions.name: Catastrophizing
distortions.distortion: Predicting an outcome then magnifying the negative; worry escalates quickly.
distortions.truth: Emotions make things seem bigger than they are; catastrophizing breeds helplessness.
reversing: What could go right? What are other possible outcomes?
What's really the worst that could happen — and how would you learn from it?

distortions.name: Fallacy of Fairness
distortions.distortion: Believing fair means equal, rather than individualized to the person or situation.
distortions.truth: Fairness gives equal opportunity, not identical outcomes; demanding equality breeds resentment.
reversing: What is needed here, versus what is 'fair'?
What if fair isn't equal — how would you handle this?

distortions.name: Filtering
distortions.distortion: Focusing on only one piece of information — usually the negative — and dwelling on it.
distortions.truth: There are multiple sides to every situation; few absolutes exist.
reversing: If you gave the person/situation the benefit of the doubt, what would you do differently?

distortions.name: Black & White Thinking
distortions.distortion: All-or-none, either/or reasoning; no middle ground or collaboration considered.
distortions.truth: There are multiple perspectives to every story; ideal state needs the tension between opposing forces.
reversing: What if the opposite were true — what would you do differently?
What value can you find in other perspectives?

distortions.name: Global Labeling
distortions.distortion: Defining a person or situation with a highly biased universal label (heightened over-generalization).
distortions.truth: Every person and situation has unique qualities beyond the label.
reversing: Even with that tendency, what CAN you do?
What's unique about this person you can leverage?

distortions.name: Being Right
distortions.distortion: Needing to always be right; believing being right determines worth or identity.
distortions.truth: It's okay to be wrong — change requires something to be re-examined; opposing perspectives can both hold truth.
reversing: What if the other perspective is also true?
How is the need to be right affecting the relationship?

distortions.name: The Should's
distortions.distortion: Unrealistic demands on self or others — “should,” “ought to,” “must,” “have to.”
distortions.truth: “Should” thinking doesn't create intrinsic motivation or lasting change; unmet ideals breed shame and guilt.
reversing: What could you do, be, or feel instead?
What if something else were true — what would you do differently?
